name: Freddie Waits
abstract: Frederick Douglas Waits (April 27, 1943 – November 18, 1989) was an American hard bop and post-bop drummer. Waits never officially recorded as leader, but was a prominent member and composer in Max Roach's M'Boom percussion ensemble.

about_quotes.speaker: Frank Foster
about_quotes.evidence: At Davidson College of Rutgers University I was one of the jazz professors under Larry Ridley, with Ted Dunbar, Kenny Barron, and the late Freddie Waits. We had a quintet called the Jazz Professors.
about_quotes.doc_title: Frank Foster — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Barron
about_quotes.evidence: We first started playing together with Freddie, Freddie Hubbard... Freddie was – he was definitely one of my favorite drummers... It was called Sunset to Dawn with – who is it? Freddie Waits.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Barron — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: I used Freddie Waits to play drums. We recorded an album called “You Had Better Listen” in 1967.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Hubert Laws
about_quotes.evidence: My first group was called the Afro-Classic Ensemble. Ron Carter was in it. I think Freddie Waits. ... Waits played on my band on it
about_quotes.doc_title: Hubert Laws — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Curtis Fuller
about_quotes.evidence: And then Freddie Waits transcribed all the solos and sent them to colleges and high schools and people did arrangements for drums.
about_quotes.doc_title: Curtis Fuller — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Tyner McCoy
about_quotes.evidence: Yourself, Herbie Lewis, Freddie Waits, Gary Bartz, which was a long-term association... [INTERVIEWEE] Yeah, I like Hubert...
about_quotes.doc_title: Tyner McCoy — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
